questions.question:
Computers are ubiquitous. They are used to improve efficiency in almost all fields from agriculture to space exploration. Artificial intelligence (AI) is currently a hot topic. AI enables computers to learn, given enough training data. For humans, sitting in front of a computer for long hours can lead to health issues.

Which of the following can be deduced from the above passage?

i. Nowadays, computers are present in almost all places.
ii. For humans, there are both positive and negative effects of using computers.
iii. Artificial intelligence can done without computers.

A. (i) and (ii)  B. (i) and (iv)  C. (i), (ii) and (v)  D. (i) and (ii)
questions.to_find: Which statements can be logically deduced from the given passage

understand.plain: The passage states computers are present in almost all fields (ubiquitous), AI helps computers learn, and long hours at computers harm health. We must identify which numbered statements follow directly from the passage without adding outside information. Statement iii says AI can be done without computers, which contradicts the passage that says AI 'enables computers to learn' — it presupposes computers. Only (i) and (ii) are supported.

keywords.explain: Present everywhere or in very many places at the same time
keywords.example: Computers are ubiquitous — found in homes, hospitals, farms, and spacecraft

keywords.explain: Logically inferred from the given information without adding outside knowledge
keywords.example: From 'all cats are mammals' we can deduce 'if Tom is a cat, Tom is a mammal'

keywords.explain: The simulation of human learning and reasoning by machines using data-driven algorithms
keywords.example: AI-powered recommendation engines learn your preferences from past behaviour

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a pure reading-comprehension question.

given.aim: Identify which numbered statements are logically supported by the passage

terms.meaning: Computers are ubiquitous — used in almost all fields
terms.connects: Directly supports statement (i): computers are present almost everywhere

terms.meaning: Long computer use can cause health problems for humans
terms.example: Sitting in front of a computer for long hours leads to health issues
terms.connects: Combined with positive effects (efficiency), this supports statement (ii): both positive and negative effects exist

terms.meaning: AI enables computers to learn given enough training data
terms.example: AI is a hot topic in computer science research
terms.connects: AI is described as working through computers, so statement (iii) — AI without computers — is contradicted by or unsupported by the passage

given.plan: Check each numbered statement against the explicit content of the passage. Accept only those that are directly entailed. Reject any statement that requires outside knowledge or contradicts the passage.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (i)
steps.apply: The passage says 'Computers are ubiquitous... used in almost all fields from agriculture to space exploration.' This directly says computers are present in almost all places. Statement (i) is TRUE.
steps.note: Ubiquitous literally means present everywhere; the passage endorses this for computers.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (ii)
steps.apply: Positive effect: computers improve efficiency. Negative effect: long hours cause health issues. Both are stated explicitly in the passage. Statement (ii) is TRUE.
steps.note: The passage itself provides one positive (efficiency) and one negative (health) effect.

steps.title: Evaluate statement (iii)
steps.apply: The passage says 'AI enables computers to learn' — it connects AI with computers, not independent of them. The claim that 'AI can be done without computers' is neither stated nor supported; in fact the passage implies AI needs computers. Statement (iii) is NOT supported.
steps.note: A deduction question requires us to stay within the passage; outside technical knowledge about AI hardware is irrelevant.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Only (i) and (ii) are deducible. The matching option is D.
steps.note: Options A and D appear similar in the printed question; in context the answer key confirms D.

solution.result: D. (i) and (ii) — only these two statements are directly supported by the passage.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas are needed; this question relies on logical inference from a text passage.

questions.question:
The importance of sleep is often overlooked by students when they are preparing for exams. Research has consistently shown that sleep deprivation greatly reduces the ability to recall the material learnt. Hence, cutting down on sleep to study longer hours can be counterproductive.

Which one of the following statements is the CORRECT inference from the above passage?

A. Sleeping well alone is enough to prepare for an exam. Studying has lesser benefit.
B. Students are not right in thinking that sleep is a waste of time.
C. If a student is extremely well prepared for an exam, he needs little or no sleep.
D. To do well in an exam, adequate sleep must be part of the preparation.
questions.answer: D. To do well in an exam, adequate sleep must be part of the preparation.
questions.to_find: The correct inference about sleep and exam preparation from the passage

understand.plain: The passage makes three claims: students often undervalue sleep, research shows sleep deprivation hurts recall, and therefore reducing sleep to study more is counterproductive. An inference must follow logically from these claims without over-extending them. Option D directly mirrors the passage's conclusion — sleep is necessary for good recall, so it must be part of exam preparation. Options A and C make extreme claims not supported by the passage, and option B, while partially true, is weaker than D.

keywords.explain: A state of insufficient sleep that impairs cognitive functions, especially memory consolidation
keywords.example: Staying awake all night before an exam is a form of sleep deprivation

keywords.explain: Having the opposite effect of what was intended; making things worse rather than better
keywords.example: Skipping sleep to study more is counterproductive if it reduces recall

keywords.explain: A conclusion drawn logically from stated evidence, not an assumption beyond the given data
keywords.example: From 'all birds have wings', we can infer 'pigeons have wings'

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a pure verbal reasoning question.

given.aim: Identify the one statement that is fully supported by the passage as a correct logical inference

terms.meaning: Sleep deprivation greatly reduces ability to recall learnt material
terms.example: Students who sleep well retain more of what they studied
terms.connects: This is the key premise — adequate sleep aids recall, which is essential for exams

terms.meaning: Cutting down sleep to study longer can be counterproductive
terms.example: More study hours with less sleep may yield lower marks
terms.connects: This is the author's direct conclusion; option D is a restatement of this conclusion

terms.meaning: The passage does not say sleep alone is sufficient, nor does it exempt well-prepared students from needing sleep
terms.example: Options A and C both go beyond what the passage claims
terms.connects: Correct inference must stay within the scope of the passage

given.plan: Test each option against the passage. Accept the one that is a direct, bounded restatement of the passage's argument. Reject any option that overclaims, underclaims, or introduces conditions not in the text.

steps.title: Evaluate option A
steps.apply: A says 'Sleeping well alone is enough.' The passage never claims sleep alone replaces studying — it only says skipping sleep is counterproductive. Option A overclaims. REJECT.
steps.note: The passage is about the necessity of sleep, not its sufficiency as the only preparation.

steps.title: Evaluate option B
steps.apply: B says students are wrong to think sleep is a waste. This is implied, but it frames the passage's message negatively (students' flawed thinking). It is less direct than option D. Partially true, but not the best inference.
steps.note: Option B is inferrable, but option D captures the constructive conclusion more precisely.

steps.title: Evaluate option C
steps.apply: C claims a well-prepared student needs little or no sleep. The passage makes no such exception — sleep deprivation reduces recall for everyone regardless of preparation level. REJECT.
steps.note: Introducing an exemption based on preparation level is beyond the scope of the passage.

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.apply: D says adequate sleep must be part of exam preparation. This is a direct, bounded restatement of the passage: sleep deprivation hurts recall, so adequate sleep is a necessary component of effective preparation. ACCEPT.
steps.note: D is the most precise direct inference from the passage's research finding and conclusion.

solution.result: D. To do well in an exam, adequate sleep must be part of the preparation.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas are required; correct inference follows from careful reading and logical evaluation of each option.

questions.question:
Crowd funding deals with mobilisation of funds for a project from a large number of people, who would be willing to invest smaller amounts through web-based platforms in the project.

Based on the above paragraph, which of the following is correct about crowd funding?

A. Funds raised through unwilling contributions on web-based platforms
B. Funds raised through large contributions on web-based platforms
C. Funds raised through voluntary contributions on web-based platforms
D. Funds raised through voluntary contributions on web-based platforms
questions.answer: D. Funds raised through voluntary contributions on web-based platforms
questions.to_find: The correct characterisation of crowd funding based on the passage

understand.plain: The passage defines crowd funding as mobilising funds from many people who 'would be willing' to invest smaller amounts through web-based platforms. The phrase 'willing to invest' means voluntary contributions, and the mechanism is explicitly web-based. The correct option must preserve both the voluntary nature and the web-based platform aspect, while reflecting that amounts are smaller (not large). Option D captures 'voluntary contributions on web-based platforms' accurately.

keywords.explain: Raising money for a project by collecting small voluntary contributions from a large number of people, typically online
keywords.example: A startup raises seed money by asking thousands of backers to contribute small sums on a platform

keywords.explain: The act of collecting or pooling financial resources from multiple sources
keywords.example: A charity mobilises funds by running an online donation campaign

keywords.explain: Internet-accessible services or websites that facilitate transactions, communication, or fund collection
keywords.example: Kickstarter and Indiegogo are well-known web-based crowdfunding platforms

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a reading comprehension question based on a definition.

given.aim: Select the option that accurately reflects the passage's definition of crowd funding

terms.meaning: Participants choose voluntarily to contribute — they are not coerced
terms.example: A backer on a crowdfunding platform decides on their own to pledge money
terms.connects: Rules out option A ('unwilling contributions') and affirms the 'voluntary' keyword in options C and D

terms.meaning: Individual contributions are modest, not large
terms.example: Each backer may give just 10-100 rupees, but thousands of them together fund the project
terms.connects: Rules out option B ('large contributions') because the passage explicitly says smaller amounts

terms.meaning: The mechanism of collecting funds is an online platform
terms.example: Funds are pooled via an internet portal accessible to investors worldwide
terms.connects: All options mention web-based platforms, so this phrase is consistent but not a differentiator

given.plan: Check each option against the two critical attributes from the passage: (1) willing/voluntary contributors and (2) smaller amounts. Eliminate options that contradict either attribute.

steps.title: Identify key attributes from the passage
steps.apply: Passage says: (a) people 'willing to invest' = voluntary, (b) 'smaller amounts', (c) 'through web-based platforms'. Any correct option must agree with all three.
steps.note: Focus on the words 'willing' and 'smaller amounts' as they distinguish the options.

steps.title: Eliminate option A
steps.apply: Option A says 'unwilling contributions'. The passage says 'willing to invest'. This directly contradicts the passage. REJECT.
steps.note: Unwilling vs willing is the clear differentiator here.

steps.title: Eliminate option B
steps.apply: Option B says 'large contributions'. The passage says 'smaller amounts'. This contradicts the passage. REJECT.
steps.note: Crowd funding's defining feature is many small contributions, not few large ones.

steps.title: Select option D
steps.apply: Options C and D both say 'voluntary contributions on web-based platforms', which matches the passage. The answer key confirms D. ACCEPT D.
steps.note: Both C and D match the passage; the official answer is D.

solution.result: D. Funds raised through voluntary contributions on web-based platforms.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this is a definition-based reading comprehension question.

questions.question:
The recent measures to improve the output would ______ the level of production to our satisfaction.

A. increase  B. decrease  C. speed  D. equalise
questions.to_find: The word that correctly fills the blank to make the sentence meaningful and grammatically accurate

understand.plain: This is a sentence-completion (fill-in-the-blank) question testing vocabulary and contextual reasoning. The sentence speaks of 'measures to improve the output' — improving output means raising it. The blank governs the level of production, which should go up to reach satisfaction. Among the choices, 'increase' is the only word that matches the positive direction implied by 'improve' and 'satisfaction'. The other choices either reduce, add speed improperly, or imply an unrelated equalising action.

keywords.explain: Actions or steps taken to achieve a goal or bring about a change
keywords.example: The government announced measures to reduce inflation

keywords.explain: The amount produced or the result of a process
keywords.example: The factory's daily output rose after the new machinery was installed

keywords.explain: To a degree that meets or exceeds expectations, implying a positive outcome
keywords.example: The project was completed to our satisfaction — it met all the targets

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a vocabulary and sentence-completion question.

given.aim: Choose the word that fits the blank so the sentence is both grammatically correct and contextually meaningful

terms.meaning: The measures are designed to make output better — i.e., raise it
terms.example: Improving output in a factory means producing more goods per shift
terms.connects: This positive context rules out 'decrease' and guides us toward 'increase'

terms.meaning: The result should be pleasing, confirming a positive or sufficient level has been achieved
terms.example: A student passes to the teacher's satisfaction when the marks are adequate
terms.connects: A satisfying level of production would be a higher level, reinforcing 'increase'

terms.meaning: The blank is the main verb of a relative clause modifying 'measures'; it must be an infinitive compatible with 'level of production'
terms.example: 'would increase the level' is grammatically well-formed
terms.connects: 'Speed' would require 'up' to work idiomatically; 'equalise' implies no gain; only 'increase' fits both grammar and meaning

given.plan: Test each option in context: does it preserve the positive intent of 'improve' and 'satisfaction', and is it grammatically natural with 'level of production'?

steps.title: Test option B: decrease
steps.apply: Measures to improve output would decrease the level of production — this contradicts the intent (improve = raise). REJECT.
steps.note: Decrease is the opposite of what improving measures should do.

steps.title: Test option C: speed
steps.apply: 'Would speed the level of production' is grammatically awkward. 'Speed up the level' would also be unusual. The phrase does not fit naturally. REJECT.
steps.note: 'Speed' typically collocates with 'speed up a process', not 'speed a level'.

steps.title: Test option D: equalise
steps.apply: Equalise implies bringing something to the same level as something else — there is no reference point for equalisation in this sentence. It also does not convey improvement. REJECT.
steps.note: Equalise requires a comparator; none is provided in the sentence.

steps.title: Test option A: increase
steps.apply: 'The measures to improve the output would increase the level of production to our satisfaction.' This is grammatically correct and contextually coherent — improving output leads to higher (increased) production. ACCEPT.
steps.note: Increase fits perfectly: it is the natural result of improvement measures and leads to a satisfying level.

solution.result: A. increase — the measures to improve output would raise (increase) the level of production to a satisfying degree.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas required; this question tests English vocabulary and contextual sentence completion.
